coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots with real-time nucleolus-monitoring capability for gene delivery and biosensing of NO2 – and pH
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qianliang
Shi, Lihong
Zhao, Jianghong
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: To achieve gene therapy, DNA, one of the most commonly used therapeutic nucleic acids, is typically employed to transfer into the nucleous. However, it remains extremely challenging to seek vectors with high transfection efficiency and low toxicity to introduce DNA into the nucleolus. Herein, we have designed and developed green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) for nucleolus targeting, gene delivery, and biosensing of NO2 – and pH. G-CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal mean using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and citric acid as precursors. As-prepared G-CDs not only possess bright green fluorescence with respectable fluorescence quantum yield of 41%, but also exhibit low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility. Interestingly, G-CDs display accurate nucleolus targeting characteristic due to the response of G-CDs to DNA in the nucleolus. More unexpectedly, G-CDs could effectively deliver exogenous ROX-labelled DNA into the nucleolus of HeLa cells, which is the first time that fluorescent CDs are used as nanocarriers to transfer DNA to nucleolus. What’s more, G-CDs have been expanded to visually monitor NO2 – and pH fluctuations in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nucleolus targeting
Gene delivery
Biosensing
NO2 –
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DNA targeting experiment in different living cells
NO2 – And pH detections in aqueous solution
Nucleolus targeting of G-CDs in living cells
NO2 – And pH determinations in HeLa cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) G-CDs’ TEM images at different scales. (D) G-CDs’ size distribution. (E) G-CDs’ AFM image. (Inset: G-CDs’ height). (F) G-CDs’ height distribution.
(A) G-CDs’ XPS full spectrum. (B–D) C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of G-CDs, respectively. (E) G-CDs’ FTIR spectrum. (F) G-CDs’ XRD pattern.
(A) UV–vis spectrum of G-CDs. (B) Excitation-emission matrix with a surface plot from distinct FL spectra of G-CDs. (C) G-CDs’ FL decay (emission of 508 nm, excitation of 420 nm).
LSCM images of different cells incubated with G-CDs under 405 nm laser excitation.
LSCM images of HeLa cells incubated by ROX-labeled DNA and ROX-labelled DNA + G-CDs. From left to right: green channel (λex/λem: 405 nm/490–590 nm), red channel (λex/λem: 547 nm/590–690 nm), overlay.

(A) FL spectra of G-CDs with different concentrations of NO2 – (Inset: the changes of G-CDs’ FL intensity with increasing NO2 – concentrations under 420 nm excitation). (B) The relationship between F/F0 and NO2 – concentrations. (C) Color changes of G-CDs solution with various concentrations of NO2 – under daylight.
(A) FL decay of G-CDs and G-CDs@NO2 – (emission of 508 nm, excitation of 420 nm). (B) UV–vis titration of G-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of NO2 – (0–70 μM).

(A) G-CDs’ FL intensity with various pH (6.6–9.6). (B) The relationship between G-CDs’ FL intensity and different pH (6.6–9.6). (C) Linear relationship between G-CDs’ FL intensity of and distinct pH (7.2–8.5). (D) The reversible cycle between G-CDs’ FL intensity and pH.
(A–B) G-CDs’ TEM image and size distribution at pH 8.2. (C) G-CDs’ TEM image and size distribution at pH 9.2.
Dynamic LSCM images of HeLa cells incubated by G-CDs + NO2 – under 405 nm laser excitation.
LSCM images of HeLa cells incubated by G-CDs under 405 nm laser excitation at pH 7.2, 8.2 and 9.2, respectively.
Schematic diagram of G-CDs’ preparation and applications in nucleolar targeting, gene delivery, and sensing of NO2 – and pH.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots with real-time nucleolus-monitoring capability for gene delivery and biosensing of NO2 – and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme S1. Schematic diagram of G-CDs’ preparation and applications in nucleolar targeting, gene delivery, and sensing of NO2 – and pH.

To achieve gene therapy, DNA, one of the most commonly used therapeutic nucleic acids, is typically employed to transfer into the nucleous. However, it remains extremely challenging to seek vectors with high transfection efficiency and low toxicity to introduce DNA into the nucleolus. Herein, we have designed and developed green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) for nucleolus targeting, gene delivery, and biosensing of NO2 – and pH. G-CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal mean using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and citric acid as precursors. As-prepared G-CDs not only possess bright green fluorescence with respectable fluorescence quantum yield of 41%, but also exhibit low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility. Interestingly, G-CDs display accurate nucleolus targeting characteristic due to the response of G-CDs to DNA in the nucleolus. More unexpectedly, G-CDs could effectively deliver exogenous ROX-labelled DNA into the nucleolus of HeLa cells, which is the first time that fluorescent CDs are used as nanocarriers to transfer DNA to nucleolus. What’s more, G-CDs have been expanded to visually monitor NO2 – and pH fluctuations in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new-type carbon nanomaterial, are considered to be quasi-spherical particles with sizes below 10 nm [1]. Recently, CDs have become a hot topic due to captivating advantages including superior photoluminescence, outstanding photostability, superb water solubility, low toxicity, easy functionalization and excellent biocompatibility [2–8]. These splendid characteristics endow CDs with extensive applications in cell imaging, biological sensing, gene delivery, fingerprints detection and catalysis [9–13].

Nucleolus, located within the nucleus, is the site of ribosomal DNA transcription by RNA polymerase I, processing of rRNA transcripts and assembly of ribosomes [14]. Monitoring the state of nucleolus is crucially significant for pathological detection of malignant lesions and accurate treatment of cancers. By virtue of high temporal and spatial resolution, fluorescence (FL) imaging technology has become an integral means of tracking nucleolus biological processes [15]. Recently, CDs have been developed as FL labelling reagent to stain nucleolus. Yi et al. constructed red emissive two-photon CDs to stain nucleolus for real-time monitoring dynamic nucleolus changes [16]. Zhang et al. synthesized green-emitting CDs to target nucleolus [17]. Shen et al. designed ratiometric fluorescent CDs to label nucleolus for precise imaging of Cathepsin B in living cancer cells [18]. However, the research of CDs-based nucleolus-targeted probes is still in its infancy, and there is much space for research.

Genetic changes in the nucleolus can lead to many human diseases, including spinal muscular atrophy, atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, knee osteoarthritis and metastatic solid tumors [19–23]. Thus, it is necessary to transmit normal genes or genes with therapeutic efficacies into the nucleolus to correct gene defects or exert therapeutic role. Nevertheless, naked genetic molecules can not be validly internalized into targeting cells on account of phagocyte uptake, rapid renal clearance, and serum nuclease susceptibility [4,24,25]. Currently, vectors for introducing gene into nucleolus have been developed. Suvanasuthi et al. employed AIDS virus envelope vector to transport plasmid DNA into the nucleolus through actin depolymerization [26]. Zhang et al. synthesized polymers of guanidine hydrochloride-N,N′-cystaminebisacrylamide and chlorhexidine-N,N′-cystaminebisacrylamide to target the nucleolus and deliver plasmid DNA into the nucleolus [27]. However, these vectors suffer from some drawbacks like low transfection efficiency and high toxicity. Thus, it is vitally important to seek vectors with high transfection efficiency and low toxicity to introduce genes into the nucleolus.

NO2 – is usually utilized as preservative to increase flavor of food [28]. The existence of moderate amount of NO2 – is beneficial. However, when NO2 – is excessively intaken or abused, NO2 – can convert normal hemoglobin in human blood into methemoglobin causing hypoxia and also interact with amides and secondary amines in the stomach to produce nitrosamines inducing cancer and high blood pressure [4,29]. In the light of WHO recommendations, everyday intake of NO2 – is supposed to be below 3 mg L–1 [30]. Hence, monitoring NO2 – becomes more and more significant for disease diagnosis. Up to now, CDs based NO2 – fluorescent probes have been developed. Jia et al. prepared orange-emitting N-doped CDs as fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode probes for NO2 – sensing [31]. Omer et al. employed amphiphilic fluorescent CDs to distinguish NO2 – and tetracycline [32]. Wang et al. designed N, B-doped CDs based fluorescent sensor for selective detection of NO2 – [33]. Additionally, pH plays an important role in various physiological processes [34]. Irregular pH changes may lead to many diseases in the body, such as neurological diseases, Alzheimer’s disease, vascular inflammatory diseases and even cancer [35–38]. Therefore, the measurement of intracellular pH is extremely meaningful for early disease diagnosis. So far, CDs-based fluorescent sensors for the simultaneous recognization of NO2 – and pH have not been explored. Hence, it is momentous to exploit extraordinarily sensitive CDs-based fluorescent sensors for NO2 – and pH sensing.

0.46 g of citric acid and 0.24 g of 3, 5-diaminobenzoic acid as precursors were added to 20 mL of ultrapure water by one-step hydrothermal mean to synthesize G-CDs. Then, mixture was transferred to 60 mL of reactor and heated at 220 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, reactor was cooled to ambient temperature. Obtained crude product was centrifuged by filtering with microporous membrane (0.22 μm) and dialyzing with dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) to obtain G-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA targeting experiment in different living cells
ce:section.ce:para: 0.4 mL of G-CDs solution (6.28 g L–1) was added to culture dish containing HeLa cells, RAW cells, A549 cells and SAY5Y cells, respectively. After 10 min of incubation, FL images were captured by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM).

Firstly, HeLa cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde for 5 min, which were treated with 0.1% Triton X-100 for 15 min to permeate cell membrane. Secondly, treated HeLa cells were washed three times with Tris-HCl buffer. Thirdly, 0.4 mL of G-CDs solution (6.28 g L–1) was introduced into culture dish. After incubating for 10 min, the above system was washed three times with Tris-HCl buffer. As a control, only Tris-HCl buffer, 25 μg mL−1 of Ribonuclease (RNase) and 25 μg mL−1 of Deoxyribonuclease (DNase) were added to different culture dishes, respectively. Finally, FL images were captured by LSCM after 3 h of incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NO2 – And pH detections in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: For NO2 – detection, 80 μL of G-CDs solution (6.28 g L–1) was added to 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.2). Subsequently, distinct concentrations of NO2 – standards were introduced. For pH monitoring, 80 μL of G-CDs solution (6.28 g L–1) was introduced into 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer with variational pH. After incubating for 60 s, FL spectra were collected under 420 nm at ambient condition.

TEM can be utilized to observe G-CDs’ size and morphology. Fig. 1 A–C demonstrate that G-CDs are quasi-spherical with favorable dispersion. Fig. 1D illustrates that G-CDs’ size distribution is in the scope of 1.0–5.0 nm and the average size is 2.16 ± 0.52 nm. AFM can be used to observe height distribution and surface morphology of G-CDs. G-CDs exhibit excellent dispersion (Fig. 1E). The illustration of Fig. 1E shows the radial height of G-CDs. G-CDs’ height range is 2.5–5.1 nm and even height is ca. 3.78 nm (Fig. 1F). TEM data are in good agreement with AFM results.

XPS can infer the composition and functional groups of G-CDs’ surface. The full spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 2 A) shows three main peaks at 281.9 eV, 396.9 eV and 528.8 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. C1s spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 2B) illustrates four peaks implying the existence of C = C (284.8 eV), C–N (286.0 eV), O = C–C (287.8 eV) and C = O (288.9 eV). N1s spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 2C) can be decomposed to three peaks, ascribing to C–N–C (398.8 eV), N–H (400.1 eV) and N–(C3) (402.1 eV). O1s spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 2D) could be fitted into three peaks, corresponding to C = O (531.8 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (533.2 eV and 533.9 eV). FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2E) can also be constructed to recognize functional groups on G-CDs’ surface. The absorption bands at 2927.3 cm−1 and 2579.3 cm−1 are assigned to N–H and C–H, respectively. The absorption peak at 1690.9 cm−1 corresponds to the existent of C = O. Two absorption bands at 1593.9 cm−1 and 1464.4 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of COO–. The characteristic peak at 1394.7 cm−1 is consistent with the bending vibration of C–H. The absorption peak at 1334.8 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C–N. The characteristic band at 1210.5 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of O–H. The absorption band at 1121.1 cm−1 verifies the presence of C–O bond. Measured data of FTIR is highly consistent with XPS results, indicating that G-CDs’ surface is covered with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups with the isoelectric point of 7.22 (Figure S1). XRD spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 2F) shows a peak at 23.3°, which reveals that G-CDs may contain graphite-like structures.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs displays two obvious absorption peaks centered at 228 nm and 309 nm, ascertaining to π-π* transition of C = C and n-π* transition of C = O, respectively. Fig. 3B presents the excitation-emission matrix with a surface plot from distinct photoluminescence spectra of G-CDs, which shows that optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs are 420 nm and 508 nm, respectively. Combined with the normalized emission spectra of G-CDs (Figure S2), red shift phenomenon may correspond to the effect of size-dependent luminescence or the distribution of emissive traps [39]. G-CDs’ average fluorescence lifetime is investigated to be 7.8363 ns (Fig. 3C). Through CIE chromaticity diagram of G-CDs (0.28, 0.65), we further measure G-CDs’ FL color at optimal excitation (Figure S3). G-CDs’ FL intensity hardly varies in different NaCl concentrations (Figure S4), indicating that G-CDs will have potential applications under physical salt conditions. Additionally, FL quantum yield of G-CDs is as high as 41% with Rhodamine 6G as a reference.

Cytotoxicity is an essential factor for biological applications. HeLa cells were selected as a model to assess G-CDs’ cytotoxicity through MTT assay. As shown in Figure S5, more than 86.7% HeLa cells survive at maximum concentration of 12.5 g L–1, implying that G-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and could be applied in biological system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nucleolus targeting of G-CDs in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes can be generally internalized into cytoplasm and then diffused into organelles [40]. To evaluate the distribution of G-CDs in organelles, we employed G-CDs to incubate different cells including HeLa, RAW, A549 and SAY5Y cells. As seen in LSCM images (Fig. 4 ), nucleoli emit bright green FL, indicating that G-CDs have favorable targeting effect on nucleolus.

To explore specific targeting ability of G-CDs to nucleolus, the digestion experiments of RNase and DNase were performed. Compared with control group (Figure S6A), nucleolus in the DNase group is completely invisible (Figure S6B) while FL of nucleolus has no obvious change in the RNase group (Figure S6C), indicating G-CDs can selectively combine with DNA instead of RNA when entering the nucleolus. Figure S6D also confirms that G-CDs can precisely bind to DNA in the nucleolus.

Considering the nucleolus targeting ability of G-CDs, G-CDs could be expected to serve as a nanocarrier to deliver exogenous DNA into the nucleolus. Firstly, an agarose gel electrophoresis assay was performed to investigate the complex efficiencies of G-CDs for DNA by observing the movement of DNA. As seen in Figure S7, free DNA could easily migrate to the opposite end. However, with increasing weight ratio of G-CDs to DNA from 1:1 to 50:1, DNA binding with G-CDs is gradually blocked. When the weight ratio of G-CDs to DNA is 50:1, the DNA migration can be completely blocked, confirming the effective loading of DNA in G-CDs. Secondly, in order to evaluate the gene delivery capability of G-CDs, delivery experiment of ROX-labeled DNA was performed in HeLa cells. The G-CDs/DNA weight ratio of 50:1 was chosen to ensure stable and efficacious delivery of DNA. As shown in Fig. 5 , no FL is found in ROX-labeled DNA incubated HeLa cells, indicating that ROX-labeled DNA fails to enter HeLa cells. However, when HeLa cells are incubated with both ROX-labeled DNA and G-CDs, the nucleoli of HeLa cells emit green and red FL under the excitation of 405 nm and 547 nm lasers, respectively, which demonstrates that ROX-labeled DNA successfully enters and enriches in nucleoli with the assistance of G-CDs vector. Additionally, transfection efficiency of ROX-labeled DNA to HeLa cells is calculated as 37.5% (Figure S8).

G-CDs’ surface is covered by amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The structure of exogenous DNA contains bases. Therefore, we infer that G-CDs have a strong binding ability with exogenous DNA based on intermolecular hydrogen bonds. To support the binding of G-CDs and exogenous DNA, zeta potential values were operated and TEM images were studied. Zeta potential of G-CDs solution is + 0.16 mV, while it drops to –11.93 mV after introducing exogenous DNA, indicating the successful combination between G-CDs and negatively charged DNA (Figure S9). What’s more, compared with G-CDs’ average size of 2.16 nm, a larger particle of 12.68 nm is measured upon adding exogenous DNA (Figure S10), which indicating that exogenous DNA induces G-CDs’ aggregation. According to above evidences, G-CDs bind exogenous DNA through intermolecular hydrogen bonds and carry exogenous DNA into the nucleolus.

We evaluated the effect of competitive substances on FL intensity of G-CDs. Figure S10 shows that G-CDs have superb selectivity to NO2 –. Thus, G-CDs can be employed to selectively detect NO2 –. Furthermore, interference experiment shows that there is not significant interference when the concentration of competitive substances is up to 150 μM (Figure S11).

FL sensing ability and colorimetric sensing ability of G-CDs to NO2 – were studied at pH 7.2. G-CDs’ FL intensity progressively decreases with increasing NO2 – concentration (Fig. 6 A), which has a superb linear relationship in the scope of 5–150 μM (Fig. 6B). The limit of detection of NO2 – is calculated as 21 nM, which is 3095 times lower than the maximum level of NO2 – (65 μM) in drinking water allowed by WHO [41]. Compared with reported probes in Table S1, the proposed NO2 – probe is more sensitive obviously. Additionally, the inset of Fig. 6A shows that bright green FL gradually decreases with the increase of NO2 – concentration from 0 to 150 μM. The color of G-CDs solution changes progressively from light yellow to orange-red under daylight when NO2 – concentration increases (Fig. 6C), showing that G-CDs can be used as colorimetric sensing platform. These phenomena are conducive to the achievement of dual-mode NO2 – sensing.

To further explore sensing mechanism of G-CDs for NO2 –, FL lifetimes of G-CDs and G-CDs@NO2 – were tested (Fig. 7 A). Average lifetimes of G-CDs and G-CDs@NO2 – are 7.8363 ns and 7.1401 ns, demonstrating static quenching process derived from the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between G-CDs and NO2 –. In addition, according to UV–vis titration (Fig. 7B), the appearance of a new absorption peak at 213 nm may result from the formation of the complex between G-CDs and NO2 – (Figure S12). The results indicate that the response mechanism of G-CDs to NO2 – is ascribed to static quenching.

The pH-responsive ability of G-CDs were investigated under 420 nm excitation. As showed in Fig. 8 A, G-CDs’ FL intensity slightly changes in the pH scope of 6.6–7.2, dramatically reduces as the pH increases from 8.2 to 9.2, and finally tends to be gentle in the pH range of 9.2–9.6. A sigmoidal curve is well fitted and pKa value is obtained as 8.02 (Fig. 8B). Surprisingly, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9912) is obtained at pH 7.2–8.5 (Fig. 8C). Besides, G-CDs’ FL intensity change with pH is reversibly cycled between 7.2 and 8.5 for 5 times of cycles (Fig. 8D), indicating that G-CDs have a considerable reversibility to pH. Above results confirm that as-acquired G-CDs are an excellent pH sensor, which can discriminate alkaline pH.

To study the pH sensing mechanism of G-CDs, G-CDs’ TEM images were executed at pH 8.2 and 9.2. Compared with mean diameter at pH 7.2, larger diameters of 3.08 nm and 4.13 nm are tested at pH 8.2 and 9.2 (Fig. 9 ), confirming that the increase of pH causes the aggregation of G-CDs. The functional groups on G-CDs’ surface can be deprotonated with increasing pH, which enhances electron-donating ability of O of carboxyl and hydroxyl, increases the interaction of hydrogen bonds between G-CDs, and further leads to G-CDs’ aggregation (Figure S13). Therefore, the sensing mechanism of G-CDs to alkaline pH can be attributed to the aggregation-induced quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NO2 – And pH determinations in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: NO2 –-responsive G-CDs’ FL imaging was documented by LSCM (Fig. 10 ). Upon introducing NO2 – into G-CDs-stained HeLa cells, green FL gradually weakens with extended time and can hardly be seen after 90 s, which is consistent with FL titration phenomenon of G-CDs to NO2 – in aqueous solution. Consequently, G-CDs can be used as high-efficiency FL sensors to track NO2 – in living cells.

HeLa cells were incubated with G-CDs at pH 7.2, 8.2 and 9.2, respectively. Fig. 11 displays that FL intensity gradually decreases with increasing pH, which is consistent with G-CDs’ FL phenomenon in diverse pH solution. Above results indicate that G-CDs can be used as pH sensor to visually monitor pH fluctuations in HeLa cells.

We have developed G-CDs using 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and citric acid as precursors by one-step hydrothermal route. Obtained G-CDs serve not only as a staining reagent to precisely stain nucleolus, but also as a nanovector to carry exogenous DNA into the nucleolus. Furthermore, G-CDs could be applied to visually monitor NO2 – and pH fluctuations in living cells. It is anticipated that proposed G-CDs, a low-toxic and biocompatible gene carrier, will exhibit attractive prospects in nucleolus-targeted gene therapy.

Qianliang Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jianghong Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.153902.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and Eco-friendly Synthesis of Green Fluorescent Carbon Nanodots for Applications in Bioimaging Patterning and Staining

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploration of pH-responsive Carbon Dots for Detecting Nitrite and Ascorbic Acid

Interfaces 11 (2019) 16822–16829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red-Emissive Carbon Dots for Fingerprints Detection by Spray Method: Coffee Ring Effect and Unquenched Fluorescence in Drying Process
